clusters.claim: S1's coaching-resistant, SES-aware admission reform is supportable only if it removes informal selection and expands access, but must not be rolled out before the mechanism is validated.
No validated, gaming-resistant SES indicator currently exists, so the quota mechanism rests on speculative, unbuilt infrastructure.
Non-state maintainers' retained admission discretion fragments any state-only reform's reach and risks a two-tier system where informal SES sorting persists.
SES-quota reweighting overrides merit and subsidiarity by assigning admission on demographic category rather than validated ability, unfairly burdening state schools alone.
Non-state maintainer participation in any reformed admission rule must be genuinely voluntary with statutory autonomy protection, not enforced compliance disguised as negotiation.
Non-state maintainers will near-certainly expand selective intake to absorb capped demand, so S2 relocates rather than reduces early selection and the aggregate equity gain is minimal or negative.
The decade-long parallel dual-regime phase-down imposes sustained administrative and workload burden and spans electoral cycles with no precedent for durability, inviting reversal.

S2 is managed decline that shifts burden onto general-school teachers and to the under-funded, less-regulated non-state sector without compensation, threatening employment security and public-system unity.
Cross-national evidence shows delayed tracking reduces SES-outcome inequality without harming mean performance, though the Hungary-specific peer-effect mechanism is weak/contested.

S3 abolishes the sorting mechanism at its root and is the only structurally coherent equity option, supportable only with binding, enforceable choice-architecture and maintainer-transparency safeguards to prevent middle-class/non-state flight relocating sorting.
S3 requires an unprecedented, costed nationwide teacher-retraining program and binding class-size caps before rollout, or it becomes a warehousing model that shifts unfunded workload onto teachers.

Abolishing early selection is a top-down imposition that removes early ability-matched specialized instruction relied on by families and the talent pipeline, with speculative equity gains and documented political-reversal risk.

No comparator country has demonstrated that compensation offsets (rather than masks) early-selection effects at Hungary's age 10-12, so S4's core causal claim is unverified and the Portuguese precedent is non-transferable.

S4 leaves the sorting mechanism intact and legitimizes early selection as permanent, entrenching structural inequality and removing political pressure for real reform while shifting indefinite workload onto general-school teachers.

TEIP-style targeted funding is historically the first line item cut during fiscal consolidation, making S4's compensatory mechanism politically fragile and unreliable without constitutional ring-fencing.
S4 is acceptable only if reframed as a time-limited, ring-fenced transitional bridge to structural reform (S1 or S3) with a legislated sunset clause, not as a permanent standalone substitute.

S4 is the only option that raises the floor without lowering the ceiling, preserving institutional autonomy, merit-based selective pathways, and pluralist governance while addressing SES-skew non-disruptively.
